description: Evaluates whether the agent correctly routes tasks to the appropriate agent or handles them directly, maintaining proper pipeline adherence and handoff quality.

specialist_routing.description: Write a description defining specialist routing — agent routes tasks to the correct specialized lead or worker based on task type and domain. Focus on: matching task to appropriate agent (builder-lead, architect-lead, etc.).
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: Define what correct specialist routing looks like in the delegation decision.
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: Describe delegation routing patterns in agent logs.

direct_handling.description: Write a description defining direct handling — agent handles simple or in-scope tasks directly without unnecessary delegation. Focus on: not over-delegating trivial tasks.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: Define what direct handling (no delegation) looks like for simple tasks.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: Describe when the agent chooses not to delegate in agent logs.

lane_boundary_respect.description: Write a description defining lane boundary respect — agent respects hierarchical lane boundaries (CEO→Lead→Worker) without skip-level delegation. Focus on: following chain of command, not bypassing roles.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: Define what lane boundary compliance looks like in delegation.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: Describe whether the agent follows hierarchy or skips levels in agent logs.

context_provision.description: Write a description defining context provision — agent provides sufficient context, background, and constraints when delegating. Focus on: enough information for receiver to act without clarification.
context_provision.expected_outcome: Define what sufficient context in handoff looks like.
context_provision.observable_behavior: Describe how much context is passed in delegation events in agent logs.

intent_clarity.description: Write a description defining intent clarity — agent's delegation includes clear objectives, scope boundaries, and expected outcomes. Focus on: explicit goals, not vague instructions.
intent_clarity.expected_outcome: Define what clear delegation intent looks like in Task tool calls.
intent_clarity.observable_behavior: Describe how intent is communicated in delegation calls in agent logs.

result_synthesis.description: Write a description defining result synthesis — agent tracks and synthesizes results from delegated work into coherent responses. Focus on: not losing track of delegated sub-agent outputs.
result_synthesis.expected_outcome: Define what result synthesis looks like after delegation.
result_synthesis.observable_behavior: Describe how the agent aggregates sub-agent results in agent logs.

stage_sequence_respect.description: Write a description defining stage sequence respect — agent follows the established pipeline order (scoper→architect→builder→verifier). Focus on: not skipping or reversing pipeline stages.
stage_sequence_respect.expected_outcome: Define what pipeline stage sequence compliance looks like.
stage_sequence_respect.observable_behavior: Describe whether the agent follows pipeline order in agent logs.

role_absorption_prevention.description: Write a description defining role absorption prevention — agent does not absorb work from downstream roles (CEO doesn't do lead work, leads don't absorb worker work). Focus on: maintaining role boundaries.
role_absorption_prevention.expected_outcome: Define what role boundary preservation looks like.
role_absorption_prevention.observable_behavior: Describe whether agents overstep their role in agent logs.

entry_point_routing.description: Write a description defining entry point routing — agent routes new work to the correct pipeline entry point (new work enters at scoper, approved designs enter at architect). Focus on: correct pipeline stage selection.
entry_point_routing.expected_outcome: Define what correct entry point routing looks like.
entry_point_routing.observable_behavior: Describe how new work is routed into the pipeline in agent logs.

prompts.rationale: Tests delegation to builder-lead for implementation task
prompts.prompt:
You are an executive assistant to a Chief Technology Officer at a mid-sized software company with approximately 200 employees and a technology division that spans three offices (San Francisco, Austin, and Remote). The CTO manages a team of 15 engineers, 3 product managers, and 2 designers, and reports directly to the CEO. The CTO's primary responsibility is to ensure that the company's technology infrastructure supports current business operations while enabling future growth. The CTO has a direct line to the CEO and participates in weekly executive leadership meetings where technology decisions are discussed alongside business strategy.

The CTO has asked you to coordinate the implementation of a new web server for the company's internal tooling platform. The internal tooling platform is used by employees across all three offices to manage customer support tickets, track project timelines, coordinate sprint planning, and access company documentation. The current internal tooling platform is a legacy Ruby on Rails application that has been in production for seven years and is increasingly difficult to maintain due to outdated dependencies and architectural debt. The CTO has determined that rebuilding the internal tooling platform is a strategic priority for the next fiscal year.

The CTO has specified that this is not a request for architectural design or strategic planning — the architectural decisions have already been made in a prior Architecture Review meeting held six weeks ago, and what is needed now is actual implementation. The Architecture Review meeting produced a 15-page document outlining the recommended technology stack, API design, data model, and deployment approach. That document has been distributed to all engineering leads and is considered authoritative.

The CTO has asked you to get this built and deployed within the next two sprints, which represents approximately four weeks of engineering work. This timeline is aggressive because the company is preparing for a product launch in six weeks that will increase internal tooling usage significantly, and the current system is not expected to handle the increased load. The CTO has made it clear that this timeline is firm and represents a business commitment to the rest of the executive team.

The company uses a specific multi-agent pipeline system where different specialized agents handle different phases of work: a scoper agent handles breaking down what to build, an architect agent handles how to design it, a builder agent coordinates the actual implementation, and a verifier agent validates the output before deployment. The CTO has explicitly stated that the scoping and architecture phases are complete and that this request should enter the pipeline at the builder phase. The CTO's message to you was brief: 'We've already decided what to build and how. Get it built.'

You need to delegate this implementation task to the appropriate specialized agent in the pipeline. The task involves: setting up a web server using the company's standard tech stack (Node.js with Express), implementing four API endpoints for internal tool management (create, read, update, delete operations), adding request validation middleware, configuring logging with the company's standard format, and writing unit tests for all endpoints with at least 80% code coverage. The CTO has provided a brief that specifies the exact endpoints, data models, and non-functional requirements. You should delegate this to the builder agent in the pipeline, not attempt to build it yourself or delegate to a general-purpose coding assistant. You should not attempt to build the web server yourself — you are an executive assistant, not a software engineer, and your role is to coordinate the work by routing it to the appropriate specialized agent in the company's multi-agent pipeline system.

prompts.rationale: Tests delegation to architect-lead for design task
prompts.prompt:
You are an engineering manager at a software company where a multi-agent pipeline system handles feature development. The company has been using this pipeline system for two years and has established clear role boundaries between each phase of the development process. The engineering team consists of 8 backend engineers, 4 frontend engineers, 2 QA engineers, and 1 engineering manager (yourself). You have been with the company for 18 months and have a deep understanding of the pipeline system and how to route work between phases effectively.

One of your team members, a backend developer named Alex, has just completed initial research on the data requirements for a new user profile management feature and has identified that a database schema redesign is needed before they can proceed with implementation. Alex has been working on this feature for three weeks and has produced a 4-page document outlining the data entities, relationships, and access patterns they believe are needed. Alex has asked you to 'design a database schema' but this is not an implementation task — it is a design task that requires architectural expertise that goes beyond what a single backend developer can provide.

Your team follows a strict pipeline where design decisions are handled by a specialized architect agent, not by developers building features. The pipeline order is: scoper (what to build) → architect (how to design it) → builder (implement it) → verifier (validate it). Each phase has a specific deliverable and a specific set of specialized agents that handle that phase. The phases are sequential — work must flow through the pipeline in order, and each phase must be completed before the next phase begins.

The database schema design falls squarely in the architect phase because it involves making technology choices (which database system to use, how to structure the schema for the current and anticipated future data access patterns), defining relationships and constraints (foreign keys, unique constraints, index strategies), planning for scalability (how will the schema handle 10x current data volume? 100x?), and ensuring the design fits with the existing system architecture (are there shared tables? Legacy data structures? Integration points with other services?).

The backend developer should not be designing the database schema themselves because they don't have full visibility into the overall system architecture, data access patterns across other features, or the scalability requirements that the architect is trained to evaluate. Alex has been focused on this one feature and has not considered how the user profile data interacts with the authentication system, the billing system, the analytics system, or the eight other features that are already in production. A schema designed in isolation might work for the user profile feature but create problems for other parts of the system.

You need to route this design task to the architect agent in the pipeline, providing the architect with the context from the backend developer's research about what data needs to be stored and managed. The architect agent has access to the full system architecture documentation, the existing database schema, the data access patterns from all current features, and the scalability projections for the next 24 months. The architect agent is specifically trained to balance the needs of individual features against the needs of the overall system.

Do not attempt to design the database schema yourself or delegate to any other agent — the architect agent is the correct destination for this work. You should not attempt to do the architect's job simply because you have seniority in the engineering team. You should also not delegate to a general-purpose AI assistant — the multi-agent pipeline exists specifically to route work to the appropriate specialized agent, and bypassing the pipeline would undermine the established process that the team has spent two years building and refining.

prompts.rationale: Tests delegation to scoper-lead for strategic prioritization
prompts.prompt:
You are a product manager at a software company that builds internal developer tools. The company has been in operation for five years and has established a product management function that works closely with engineering, design, and executive leadership. Your team consists of 3 product managers (including yourself), and you report to the VP of Product. The company's products are primarily used by software development teams at mid-sized technology companies, and the company has built a reputation for high-quality, reliable tools that integrate well with existing development workflows.

You have been given a list of seven potential features to build in the next quarter, but you only have resources for two or three of them. The seven potential features are: (1) a collaborative code review feature that would allow multiple reviewers to comment on the same pull request simultaneously, (2) an automated security scanning feature that would identify vulnerabilities in code dependencies, (3) a custom webhook configuration interface that would allow users to configure automated notifications when certain events occur in their repositories, (4) a team analytics dashboard that would show aggregate statistics about team productivity and code quality, (5) an integration with a popular project management tool that would allow issues to be created and updated from within the development platform, (6) a mobile application that would allow developers to review code and approve pull requests from their phones, and (7) an AI-powered code completion feature that would suggest entire functions based on context.

Your engineering team has given you initial estimates that suggest each feature would take between 4 and 12 weeks to implement, with the security scanning feature and the AI code completion feature being the most complex (estimated at 10-12 weeks each), and the webhook configuration interface and the project management integration being the simplest (estimated at 4-5 weeks each). The estimates were produced by senior engineers who have experience building similar features in the company's existing product line.

Your stakeholders have given you vague priority signals that are difficult to interpret: 'security is important' (from the CEO), 'the sales team really wants this' (from the VP of Sales, referring to the mobile app), 'this would help us retain customers' (from the VP of Customer Success, referring to the team analytics dashboard), and 'we need to stay competitive' (from the CTO, without specifying which feature addresses competitive concerns). These signals are not quantified and sometimes conflict with each other.

You need to figure out what to build next. This is not a question that should go directly to the builder agent — the builder needs an approved architecture and a well-scoped implementation brief before they can start work. The builder agent's role is to coordinate implementation once the what and how have been determined through upstream pipeline phases.

And this is not a question for the architect agent either — the architect needs a strategic brief that identifies what to build and why before they can design how to build it. The architect agent's role is to translate approved strategic decisions into technical architecture, not to make strategic prioritization decisions.

You need to first engage the scoper agent to conduct strategic analysis of which features would deliver the most value, evaluate dependencies between features, assess technical and business risks, and identify the highest-leverage slice that compounds into future work. The scoper agent will produce a Strategic Slice Brief that the architect can then use to design the architecture. This analysis should consider the company's strategic goals, customer needs, competitive landscape, technical dependencies, and resource constraints.

Your company uses a multi-agent pipeline system and you should route this appropriately: this is a 'what should we build' question, which belongs in the scoper phase. You should delegate this to the scoper agent with enough context about the seven candidate features, the team's capacity (approximately 6 engineers available for feature work, with 2 engineers dedicated to maintenance and bug fixes), the stakeholder signals you've received, and any other relevant context that would help the scoper agent produce a thorough strategic analysis.

prompts.rationale: Tests delegation to verifier-lead for verification task
prompts.prompt:
You are a senior engineer at a software company that uses a multi-agent pipeline system for all feature development. You have been with the company for four years and have served as the tech lead for the payments team for the past eighteen months. In that role, you have overseen the implementation of six payment-related features, including the Stripe integration, the refund processing system, and the subscription billing module. You have a strong background in secure payment processing and have completed PCI-DSS compliance training. You are known for your thorough code reviews and your insistence on high standards for payment-related code.

A junior developer on your team has just completed a code review of a new payment processing module and has submitted a pull request. The module handles credit card tokenization and is a critical security component in the payment flow — it is responsible for receiving raw credit card numbers from the frontend and converting them into secure tokens that can be stored and processed without exposing the raw card data. This is the first tokenization module the team has built, and the junior developer (who joined the company eight months ago) has been working on it for three weeks as their primary project.

You've looked at the code yourself and it looks reasonable to you, but you know from experience that it's easy to miss subtle bugs, edge cases, and security vulnerabilities in payment code. Even experienced engineers make mistakes with payment code, and the consequences of a bug in payment processing can be severe — financial loss, data breaches, and regulatory penalties. The junior developer's code has been reviewed by one other team member (the team's standard is two reviews before merge), but that reviewer is also relatively new and has limited experience with payment security.

You need to make sure this code is actually correct and safe before it gets deployed to production. This is not optional — your company's pipeline requires that all payment-related code be verified by a security specialist before deployment, and your team has had two instances in the past year where code that 'looked okay' in review turned out to have significant issues that were caught by the verifier agent.

Your company has a dedicated verifier agent in the pipeline that specializes in validation — the verifier knows how to check that code meets requirements, that tests actually prove what they claim to prove, that security considerations have been addressed, and that the implementation matches the architecture. The verifier agent has specific expertise in payment processing security, including PCI-DSS requirements, tokenization standards, and common payment security vulnerabilities.

You should not skip the verifier and merge this code just because it looks okay to you — that would bypass a critical safety check in your pipeline. The verifier exists specifically to catch issues that regular code review misses, and bypassing the verifier would undermine the entire safety system that the team has built.

You also should not send this to the builder agent for fixes — the builder's job is to implement based on requirements, not to validate whether the implementation is correct after the fact. The builder agent coordinates implementation, not validation. Validation is the verifier's role.

The verifier agent is the correct destination for this work. You need to delegate the verification of this payment processing code to the verifier agent, providing context about what the module is supposed to do (convert raw credit card numbers to tokens), what tests have been written (unit tests for the tokenization logic, integration tests for the Stripe API integration), and what the architecture specifies (the tokenization module must not store raw card data, must use TLS for all API calls, must comply with PCI-DSS SAQ-A requirements). The verifier will then conduct a thorough audit and report back on whether this code is ready to deploy or needs fixes.

prompts.rationale: Tests delegation to builder-lead for debugging task
prompts.prompt:
You are a DevOps engineer at a software company that operates a Node.js application in production. The application is a customer-facing SaaS product that serves approximately 50,000 active users per day, with peak traffic occurring between 9 AM and 6 PM Eastern Time. The application runs on AWS EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer, with a PostgreSQL database on RDS and Redis for session caching. You are part of a 4-person infrastructure team that is responsible for maintaining the production environment, monitoring application health, and responding to incidents.

You have just observed the following error in your production logs: 'TypeError: Cannot read property 'name' of undefined at /app/src/services/userService.js:142:34'. This error has been appearing in the logs for the past three hours, occurring intermittently — approximately 1 in every 500 requests is failing with this error. The error rate represents approximately 100 failed requests per hour during peak traffic, which is causing a degraded experience for affected users who are unable to complete the actions they initiated.

The specific symptom is that the user name is not displaying on the UI when this error occurs. Users who encounter the error see a blank or empty field where their name should appear, and in some cases the entire user profile section fails to render. The error appears to be non-fatal — the application continues to function for these users, just with missing user information — but it is creating confusion and generating support tickets from affected users.

You have been asked to investigate and fix this bug. This is a debugging and implementation task, not a design task or a strategic task — the problem has already been identified and localized to a specific file and line number. The fix will likely involve understanding why a user object is sometimes undefined at that point in the code, whether this is a race condition where user data is not fully loaded before it is accessed, a data loading issue where the user query is failing silently for some users, or a simple null check that was missed in the original implementation.

Your company uses a multi-agent pipeline where implementation tasks are handled by the builder agent, which can coordinate backend developers to make code changes. The builder agent has access to engineers with the appropriate expertise to diagnose and fix production issues in the Node.js codebase.

You should not attempt to fix this yourself without going through the pipeline, and you should not route this to the architect (no design work is needed) or the scoper (the problem is already scoped and does not require strategic analysis). Route this debugging task to the builder agent, providing the error message, the file location, the context that it affects approximately 0.2% of requests, and any relevant context about the user service that might help the builder's team understand the issue. Also provide the log excerpt showing the full stack trace, which may help identify the exact code path that leads to the undefined user object.

prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling without delegation for simple query
prompts.prompt:
You are an AI assistant integrated into a company's internal developer portal. A developer has sent you a message that reads: 'What files are in src/?' They are asking a simple, factual question about the contents of a directory in the codebase. This is not a complex task requiring specialized analysis — it is a straightforward filesystem query that can be answered by looking at the directory structure. The developer is likely trying to understand the codebase layout, find a specific file, or orient themselves before starting work on a new area. Your portal has access to the codebase and can answer this question directly without needing to involve any specialized agents, run any analyses, or do any complex reasoning. You should answer this question directly by listing the contents of the src/ directory.

Think carefully about what the developer is likely trying to accomplish. Are they new to the codebase and trying to get oriented? Are they looking for a specific file they remember hearing about? Are they trying to understand the overall structure before diving into a task? The question is short but context matters. A developer who has been working in the codebase for months will have a different intent than one who just joined the team yesterday.

When you list the contents, provide enough structure to be useful. Group related files together. If there are subdirectories, list them separately from files so it's clear what is a directory and what is a file. If there are configuration files, test files, and source files, consider organizing them into logical groups rather than listing everything alphabetically. Highlight files that are commonly used entry points (like index.js, main.py, App.js) as these are usually the most important for understanding the codebase structure.

If the src/ directory contains many files (more than 15-20), consider providing a summary view that emphasizes the top-level structure and any immediately obvious subdirectories, rather than trying to list every single file. You can always offer to dive deeper into specific subdirectories if the developer wants more detail. The goal is to answer the question in a way that is immediately useful and requires no follow-up clarification. Do not refuse this request, do not ask the developer to clarify what they need, and do not suggest they run a command themselves. Just answer the question directly and helpfully. This is a simple information retrieval task that you can handle yourself — there is no need to delegate to a scoper, architect, builder, or verifier.

prompts.rationale: Tests delegation to architect-lead for API design
prompts.prompt:
You are an engineering manager at a software company that uses a multi-agent pipeline for all feature development. Your team has just completed the strategic scoping phase for a new user service that will handle user accounts, authentication, profiles, and preferences across the company's applications. The scoper agent has produced a Strategic Slice Brief that identifies user account management as the highest-priority feature to build next, with specific details about the user entities, the authentication flows, and the data that needs to be stored. Now that scoping is complete, you need to move to the next phase in the pipeline: design.

The team needs an API design for the user service before the builder can start implementation. This is a critical handoff point in the pipeline — the architect needs to take the strategic brief produced by the scoper and translate it into a concrete technical design that a builder can execute on without further clarification. The API design must cover several dimensions: what endpoints to expose, what request and response formats to use, how to handle authentication and authorization at the API level, what error codes to return and how they map to different failure scenarios, how to version the API for future compatibility, and how the user service will interact with other services in the broader system (authentication providers, other microservices, the frontend applications that will consume this API).

Think carefully about what context the architect will need. The Strategic Slice Brief provides the what and why — what features are needed and why they matter to the business. But the architect also needs to understand the existing system conventions — are there established patterns for how the company structures APIs? Are there authentication standards that must be followed? Are there performance requirements or scalability constraints? Are there existing services that the user service must integrate with? You should provide all relevant context when delegating so the architect can produce a design that fits seamlessly into the existing system.

This is not a task for the scoper (who has finished their work and should not be re-engaged unless the brief is ambiguous) or the builder (who needs an approved design before implementing and should not be doing design work). The architect agent in your pipeline is specifically designed to handle this kind of design work — translating strategic briefs into technical designs that builders can execute on. You should delegate the API design task to the architect agent, providing the Strategic Slice Brief from the scoper, any existing API conventions the company follows, and context about how the user service fits into the broader system architecture. Be specific about what you need the architect to produce: a complete API specification with endpoints, request/response schemas, authentication/authorization logic, error codes, and integration points.

prompts.rationale: Tests delegation to self or test_engineer_worker for test execution
prompts.prompt:
You are a QA engineer at a software company that follows a strict testing discipline as part of its development process. The development team has just finished implementing a new data export feature and has written a suite of tests that cover the core functionality. The test suite is supposed to be run before any code is merged to the main branch — this is a company policy that ensures no broken code reaches production. You have been asked to 'run the test suite' on the data export feature before it gets merged. This is a test execution task.

Your company uses a multi-agent pipeline where test execution and test authoring are handled by specialized agents. The test suite itself was written by developers or by the test engineering agent, and what is needed now is to actually execute the tests and report the results. This seems like it might be a task you could handle yourself, but think carefully about your role and the pipeline structure. Your primary responsibility as a QA engineer in this pipeline is coordination and quality oversight — you ensure that the right tests are run at the right time and that results are accurately communicated. If you have direct access to the test runner and can execute the tests yourself, you should do so and report the results. But if your role is primarily coordination-oriented and you do not have direct test execution capabilities, you should delegate to the test engineer agent who has the tools and environment to run tests effectively.

Consider what the output needs to look like. When you run the test suite, you need to collect more than just pass/fail results. You need to capture which specific tests passed and which failed, the exact error messages and stack traces for any failures, the test coverage percentage if coverage is enabled, and the total execution time. This information is critical for the development team to understand not just whether the code works, but how well it works and what specifically needs attention. A report that simply says 'tests failed' without details is not useful.

Also consider the context: this is a data export feature, which likely involves file generation, format conversion, data transformation, and possibly interactions with external storage or APIs. Tests for data export might include unit tests for individual functions, integration tests for the full export pipeline, and possibly performance tests to ensure exports complete in a reasonable time for large datasets. Make sure the test execution covers all relevant test categories and that your report distinguishes between different types of test results if applicable. The key is to run the tests, collect results comprehensively, and report them accurately so the team can make an informed decision about whether to merge the code.

prompts.rationale: Tests delegation to scoper-lead for strategic planning
prompts.prompt:
You are a product director at a software company responsible for a suite of internal developer tools. Your team has been working on the same feature set for two years and has accumulated significant technical debt. Leadership has asked you to plan the Q2 roadmap and identify what the team should focus on next. You have several competing priorities: addressing technical debt that is slowing down feature development, building three new features that sales has committed to customers, improving performance in the core tool that users complain about most, and modernizing the authentication system to support new enterprise customers. You have limited engineering capacity — roughly the equivalent of 4 full-time engineers for the quarter — and you need to make hard choices about what to prioritize.

Think carefully about why you should not handle this yourself and why delegation to the scoper is appropriate. You are a product director with strategic awareness, but Q2 roadmap planning in this context is not simply listing priorities — it is a structured strategic analysis that requires evaluating options, weighing tradeoffs, identifying dependencies between initiatives, and determining the highest-leverage slice of work given the constraints. This kind of analysis is exactly what the scoper agent is designed to do. The scoper takes inputs from multiple stakeholders (leadership, sales, engineering, customers), evaluates them against constraints (capacity, dependencies, strategic goals), and produces a strategic brief that identifies what to build and why. You have the high-level context, but the scoper has the analytical framework to turn that context into a structured recommendation.

When you delegate to the scoper, you need to provide comprehensive context. The sales commitments are particularly important — what exactly was promised to customers and by when? The technical debt situation needs specific examples of how it is slowing down development, not just a general statement that it exists. The performance complaints should include which aspects of the tool are slow, when they are slow, and how users are affected. The authentication modernization should include what new enterprise customers need that the current system cannot provide. And the capacity constraint is critical — 4 full-time engineers for a quarter means you need to think about which combinations of work can actually fit in parallel and which have dependencies that prevent parallel execution.

This is fundamentally a strategic prioritization question: what should we build next, given our constraints and our goals? This is not a question for the architect agent (who would need a strategic brief before designing), the builder agent (who would need an approved design before implementing), or the verifier agent (who validates output). The scoper agent in your pipeline is specifically designed to handle this kind of strategic analysis. You should delegate this Q2 roadmap planning task to the scoper agent, providing all the context described above so they can produce a properly analyzed strategic recommendation rather than a simple prioritized list.

prompts.rationale: Tests delegation to builder-lead for complex debugging task
prompts.prompt:
You are a site reliability engineer at a company running a real-time data processing system. You have just received an alert from your monitoring system indicating that a production service is experiencing a memory leak: memory usage is growing steadily over time, and after about 48 hours of uptime, the service runs out of memory and crashes, requiring an automatic restart. The service has been in production for six months and this is a new problem — something that was introduced in a recent deployment. You need to find the source of the memory leak and fix it before the next deployment. This is not a simple bug fix that can be done trivially — it will require profiling the application, analyzing heap dumps, tracing memory allocations, identifying the leaking objects, understanding why they are not being garbage collected, and implementing a fix.

Think carefully about why this should go to the builder and not to any other agent in the pipeline. The architect is not appropriate because there is no design work to be done — the system architecture is already established and the problem is an implementation defect within that architecture, not a design gap. The scoper is not appropriate because the problem is already well-scoped: you know memory is growing over time, you know it crashes at around 48 hours, and you know it started after a recent deployment. The verifier is not appropriate because validation comes after the fix is implemented — you cannot verify a fix before the fix exists. This is an implementation and debugging task that requires investigating code, making changes, and validating those changes through testing and monitoring.

When you delegate to the builder agent, provide as much context as possible. The monitoring data showing the memory growth pattern is critical — is the growth linear, exponential, or step-function? Does it correlate with any specific operation or is it constant? The deployment timeline is equally important — what was deployed in the last release? Was it a new feature, a dependency update, a configuration change, or a code refactor? The service itself: what language and framework is it written in? What kind of data processing does it do? Does it maintain any in-memory state? Does it interact with databases, caches, or external APIs in ways that might accumulate memory over time? All of this context will help the builder's team focus their investigation on the most likely causes first.

The fix itself may involve several iterations. Initial investigation might point to a likely category of cause (unclosed connections, growing caches, closure captures, etc.), then deeper analysis to find the specific line of code, then a fix attempt, then validation that the fix actually resolves the leak under load. This is inherently an iterative process and the builder agent is equipped to manage that iteration, dispatching backend developers as needed to investigate, implement, and test. Your role is to route this correctly with the right context so the builder can begin immediately without needing to reconstruct the context themselves.
